data.description:
Market Review

  • For the 3-month period ending March 31, 2025, the MSCI EAFE Index rose 6.9% (in USD). Performance was broad, with nine of eleven sectors posting gains. The best performing sectors were financials (up 15.2%), energy (up 15.2%), and utilities (up 12.5%). The worst performing sectors were information technology (down 2.8%), consumer discretionary (down 0.7%), and real estate (up 1.3%). The five largest countries within the benchmark all rose during the quarter, with Japan up 0.3%, United Kingdom up 9.7%, France up 10.3%, Switzerland up 11.4%, and Germany up 15.5%.
  • Regionally, Europe delivered the strongest returns. Stocks were buoyed by investor enthusiasm around the continent's pledge to increase defense spending and Germany's fiscal pivot from austerity to stimulus. These positives outweighed the negative overhang of looming tariffs, at least temporarily. The information technology sector performed the worst, pressured by worries around the secular artificial intelligence theme. Purportedly cheaper and more efficient large language models have been developed by Chinese companies such as DeepSeek and investors continue to question the ultimate return on investment of massive capital expenditures by the hyperscalers.

Portfolio Highlights

  • The NYLI Epoch International Choice Fund (the “Fund”) generated a return of 8.7% in Q1, beating the MSCI EAFE Index by 180 basis points. The relative outperformance was due to security selection, particularly within financials, industrials, consumer discretionary, and communication services. Negative security selection within health care and real estate, partially offset these positive results. Sector allocation was modestly negative, with no single sector standing out.
  • Stock selection by country contributed to relative results with notable strength in the United Kingdom, Netherlands, and Japan. The allocation by country was negative, primarily due to underweight positioning to Switzerland, Spain, and Italy.

What Helped:

    • The top three contributors to total return were European financial companies: Lloyds Banking Group (OTCPK:LLDTF)(LYG), BNP Paribas (OTCQX:BNPQF)(OTCQX:BNPQY), and NN Group (OTCPK:NNGPF)(OTCPK:NNGRY). In general, European financials benefitted from improving investor sentiment, driven by geopolitical developments including announcements of increased government spending.
    • Lloyds Banking Group is a U.K.-focused retail bank. The company reported good financial results and a strong outlook for the year ahead. Shares also benefitted from the U.K. financial regulator intervening in a lawsuit over unfair loan pricing in the motor finance market. Lloyds has a strong balance sheet and offers an attractive shareholder yield, in the form of ordinary dividends and share buybacks.
    • BNP Paribas is France's leading financial services company. The company reported better than expected 2024 results and upgraded its expectations for return on equity over the medium-term. Like Lloyds, the company offers an attractive shareholder yield with a well above market dividend yield enhanced by share repurchases.
    • NN Group is an insurance company based in the Netherlands, offering both life and property & casualty policies. The company's full year results showed a much better than expected capital position at year-end, supporting an attractive dividend and share buyback program.

What Hurt:

    • The bottom three detractors to total return were Novo Nordisk (NVO)(OTCPK:NONOF), Rohto Pharmaceutical (OTCPK:RPHCF), and Vonovia (OTCPK:VNNVF)(OTCPK:VONOY).
    • Novo Nordisk is a global pharmaceutical company specializing in diabetes and obesity care. Shares have been under pressure as weekly prescription data suggests the company's GLP-1 drugs are under- performing competing products from Eli Lily. However, the data is clouded by the presence of compounding pharmacies, who are making copy-cat versions of Novo's drugs. Compounders have been ordered by the Food and Drug Administration ((FDA)) to stop by mid-April, which should provide greater clarity on the underlying prescription trends.
    • Rohto Pharmaceutical is a Japanese manufacturer of over-the-counter skincare products, eye care products, and internal medicines. Shares were weak as the company reported disappointing fiscal third quarter results that called into question its ability to meet full year guidance. Management insists year-to-date results have been impacted by one-off issues and the phasing of expenses. The company expects to meet full year expectations.
    • Vonovia is a residential real estate developer and operator in Germany. The German government's decision to relax spending, deficit, and debt rules led to a rise in German bund yields. Investors tend to view rising yields as negative for real estate asset valuation, pressuring shares of the company. We think this view overlooks the tightness of the real estate market, with high occupancy and renewal rates.

Outlook and Positioning

  • The escalating tariff war has injected a lot of uncertainty and volatility into financial markets. The ultimate impact on the real economy and corporate fundamentals is largely dependent on the duration and magnitude of tariffs globally. While impossible to predict with any degree of precision, we expect global economic growth will be slower and inflation higher while corporate profitability will likely be negatively impacted by lower demand and tariff-induced margin pressures.
  • We continue to believe in constructing a well-diversified portfolio with an emphasis on stock specific risk. We believe the companies we own are well positioned to weather this storm. Businesses with strong competitive advantages that generate free cash flow, are appropriately capitalized, and run by excellent management teams are well prepared to navigate a lower growth, higher inflation environment. As we assess the portfolio's exposure to tariff risks, we find we own names across the spectrum of sensitivity. This is by design because neither we, nor anyone else, knows how this is all going to play out. We don't want to make a big bet on the ultimate outcome given a wide range of possibilities that span from a quick resolution and risk-on market to a global recession and further downside pressure.
  • While things feel very uncomfortable and perhaps even scary to some, our experience suggests that this too will pass. The current market environment feels very much like the Global Financial Crisis (GFC) in 2008 and the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020. Ultimately, both episodes proved to be attractive entry points and buying opportunities for patient, long-term investors. We are seeing such opportunities emerge once again. The current market drawdown is giving us the opportunity to add to names that we fundamentally like at attractive valuations.
  • It is during moments like this that our investment philosophy and process matter most. Our investment philosophy remains unchanged, with a focus on free cash flow and capital allocation. We remain committed to managing our clients' assets against this tumultuous backdrop with rigor, discipline, and the humility to accept that the future is unknowable, but our philosophy and process can help guide the way.

The opinions expressed are those of Epoch Investment Partners, Inc. as of the date of this report and are subject to change. There is no guarantee that any forecast made will come to pass. This material does not constitute investment advice and is not intended as an endorsement of any specific investment.

data.title: EIPI: Pipeline To High Yields?
data.description:
As a market junkie, I'm always looking for new funds to review, to see what the best and brightest minds of finance are coming up with. The FT Energy Income Partners Enhanced Income ETF (NYSEARCA:EIPI) recently caught my eye, as the fund boasts an attractive $0.125 / month distribution, which annualizes to 7.7% based on the current share price of ~$19.50 (Figure 1).

Is EIPI's distribution sustainable, and are the fund's fundamentals attractive?

In my opinion, EIPI's distribution is sustainable, as the fund has earned ~9.0% average annual returns since inception, which more than covers the monthly distributions.

However, EIPI's strategy of selling covered calls on ~40% of its portfolio limits the fund's upside capture, leading to the EIPI ETF underperforming passive energy infrastructure ETFs.

With distribution yields roughly equivalent to passive energy infrastructure ETFs, I don't see the long-term appeal of the EIPI ETF. I rate it a hold.

Fund Overview

The FT Energy Income Partners Enhanced Income ETF ("EIPI") is an exchange-traded fund ("ETF") launched by First Trust that is sub-advised by Energy Income Partners ("EIP"). EIP is a boutique energy investment specialist that invests primarily in "well-managed, publicly traded energy companies that own natural monopolies or provide services under long-term contracts that deliver stable and growing earnings." EIP's investment focus is on companies that can deliver high total returns through a combination of yield, growth, and capital appreciation (Figure 2).

In addition to investing in a portfolio of energy equities, the EIPI fund also employs covered call strategies on a portion of the portfolio to enhance the overall income of the fund (Figure 3).

The EIPI ETF was formed from the May 2024 amalgamation of several First Trust closed-end funds ("CEFs"), including the First Trust Energy Income and Growth Fund, the First Trust MLP and Energy Income Fund, the First Trust New Opportunities MLP & Energy Fund, and the First Trust Energy Infrastructure Fund. As a result of the mergers, the EIPI ETF has assumed the performance history of the First Trust Energy Infrastructure Fund (the "Predecessor Fund"), which had an inception date of September 27, 2011.

The EIPI ETF has over $900 million in assets under management ("AUM"), and charges a relatively high 1.11% total expense ratio (Figure 4).

Portfolio Holdings

EIPI's portfolio currently has 113 holdings, with the top 10 holdings accounting for 38.6% of the portfolio (Figure 5). EIPI's top holdings are predominantly energy infrastructure companies like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and integrated energy companies like Exxon Mobil (XOM).

The fund has also over-written 43% of the portfolio with out-of-the-money ("OTM") calls, with roughly a month in expiration (Figure 6).

Fund Performance

Historically, the EIPI ETF has performed well, with a 3-year, 5-year, and since-inception NAV average annual returns of 10.6%, 16.9%, and 9.0%, respectively (Figure 7).

The EIPI's low 10-year average return (5.5%) appears to be a timing issue, as the beginning of the period coincided with the 2014-2016 oil supply glut, which saw WTI crude prices plunge to a low of ~$26 / barrel (Figure 8).

Distribution

Considering the EIPI ETF has been able to generate long-term average annual returns of ~9.0% and the fund's $0.125 / share monthly distribution (7.7% yield), I believe EIPI's distribution is sustainable.

One way we can check distribution sustainability is to look at a fund's long-term net asset value per share ("NAV/share") (Figure 9). While EIPI's NAV does show volatility associated with its energy-equities risk, it does not show the amortizing NAV and share price pattern that is typical of amortizing return of principal funds like the Eaton Vance Limited Duration Income Fund (NYSE:EVV), which pays more than it earns (Figure 9).

EIPI, AMLP, Or XLE?

While EIPI's returns have been sufficient to fund its distribution, another question we should ask is whether EIPI ETF is the best expression of the underlying strategy of owning high-yielding energy equities.

For example, investors interested in energy equities with yield can consider the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) or the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE), both of which charge significantly lower fees than the EIPI (Figure 11).

Furthermore, energy equities in general pay higher-than-average dividends, with the AMLP ETF paying a trailing 7.9% distribution yield and the XLE ETF paying a 3.4% distribution yield, compared to EIPI's trailing 8.8% distribution yield (EIPI paid $0.2295 / share in short-term capital gains distribution in December 2024, leading to a trailing distribution yield higher than its 7.7% forward yield) (Figure 12).

At the end of the day, the single best comparison tool we should use is total returns, which captures both distributions and capital gains. Comparing the total returns between the EIPI, AMLP, and XLE ETFs, we can see that on a 5-year basis, EIPI has lagged significantly relative to AMLP and XLE (Figure 13).

This is most likely due to EIPI's strategy of selling upside returns, which limits the upside capture in EIPI's portfolio, especially after significant drawdowns like the 2020 COVID-19 market plunge.

A long-term comparison shows a similar pattern of underperformance by the EIPI ETF and its predecessor funds (Figure 14).

Where EIPI may outperform is if the underlying energy stocks were rangebound, like in the past year. In this scenario, EIPI's strategy of selling covered call options may boost its returns relative to the passive buy-and-hold strategies of AMLP and XLE (Figure 15).

Conclusion

The FT Energy Income Partners Enhanced Income ETF is an energy-equities-focused ETF that sells covered calls on a portion of its portfolio.

The EIPI ETF pays an attractive 7.7% distribution yield that appears to be sustainable, given the fund's long-term earnings power.

However, relative to vanilla passive ETFs that hold energy infrastructure assets or high-yielding energy equities, the EIPI ETF's long-term returns lag significantly due to its strategy trading away some portfolio upside for premium income.

With the uncapped AMLP ETF yielding 7.9% on a trailing basis, I don't see why investors should buy the EIPI ETF with a cap on ~40% of their portfolio returns for roughly the same distribution. I rate EIPI a hold.

data.title: NYLI MacKay Securitized Income ETF Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • The first quarter of 2025 ushered in a new era of uncertainty and volatility. By late March, we experienced an abrupt shift in investor sentiment following aggressive trade policy announcements from the U.S. administration.
  • We started the year with a consensus view on strong U.S. growth and weak inflation, but that view has since shifted to weaker growth and higher inflation.
  • Although recession odds were initially limited coming into the year, at the time of this writing, most market forecasters increased their probabilities significantly following Trump’s tariff announcements on Liberation Day April 2nd.
  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) remained on hold with markets initially pricing in two cuts in 2025. Pricing extended to four cuts once the degree and magnitude of the planned tariffs were clarified.
  • Spreads across sectors generally widened in the quarter, led by underperformance from lower quality issuers; cyclicals lagged on expectations for slower growth.
  • The Treasury yield curve bull steepened as rates fell further along the front-end. Fed Funds and 2-year rates inverted, which is unsustainable, in our view.

Portfolio Highlights

  • During the reporting period, the NYLI MacKay Securitized Income ETF outperformed relative to its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Securitized Bond Index. Portfolios with exposure to high quality, defined cash flow securities such as collateralized mortgage obligations fared better relative to unsecured credit risk. Rate volatility neither contributed nor detracted given the Fund maintained a neutral duration posture.
  • We have not engaged in any material portfolio repositioning, but we have reduced marginal risk in sectors that have performed very well over the last couple of years. We believe spreads will continue to widen near- term, and this will eventually present compelling value for re-entry. Although we are not ready to add risk at these levels, we are evaluating securities that have been “thrown out with the bath water” while we continue to sell names most directly impacted by the new tariffs (e.g. consumer cyclicals).
  • We maintain that relatively high absolute yields in fixed income combined with lower Treasury rates should still provide a buffer against near-term spread widening.

Outlook and Positioning

  • March and early April brought a whirlwind of trade policy pronouncements from the Trump administration, leading at times to significant de-risking in financial markets. At one point, the administration had announced tariffs that, if fully implemented, would have increased the effective tariff rate on goods imports by roughly 30 percentage points, to a level not seen since the 1870s. At time of writing, however, President Trump has announced a 90-day pause in his reciprocal tariffs excluding those on China, to allow time for bilateral trade negotiations. While this latest news reduces near-term recession risks, tariffs are still very likely to depress growth and raise prices in the near term. Chinese imports now face a 125 percent tariff rate; countries that faced new tariffs on April 2 will avoid the worst outcomes but will still face a 10 percent tariff; and many Canadian and Mexican products will still be tariffed at 25 percent. Sector-specific tariffs also remain in effect, while new ones are set to be rolled out shortly. Thus despite the 90-day reprieve on the most extreme tariff outcomes, U.S. households and business will still face meaningfully higher costs on imported items. In addition, uncertainty over trade policy will remain elevated for the foreseeable future, weighing on optimism and dampening discretionary household spending as well as business hiring and capital expenditures.
  • Higher tariff rates and ongoing trade policy uncertainty raise complications for monetary policy. With growth likely to slow substantially from 2024’s strong pace, the Fed will nonetheless be hesitant to cut rates preemptively given that tariffs will drive inflation higher this year. Still, once weaker growth becomes apparent and the unemployment rate begins to rise, we see scope for moderate rate cuts provided that inflation expectations remain well-anchored. Policymakers can also argue that the weaker growth outlook implies less inflation pressure once tariff effects are incorporated into prices. Still, we would not anticipate an aggressive rate cutting cycle. Inflation risks will remain elevated, and fiscal policy looks sets to loosen by the third quarter, once Congress finalizes measures to increase defense and border spending, extends expiring provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, and introduces additional tax breaks. As such, cautiously bringing the policy rate down towards a neutral setting over the next 12-18 months seems the most likely course of action.

data.title: NYLI Winslow Large Cap Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • In the first three months of the year, U.S. equities pulled back following strong gains in 2024. Volatility spiked after Chinese Artificial Intelligence (AI) research lab DeepSeek released its model that was purportedly faster and cheaper to produce than its competitors and a mix of economic concerns and policy developments weighed on investor sentiment. Additionally, investors were focused on pending tariff news. Performance of the ‘Magnificent 7’ (Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), NVIDIA (NVDA), Amazon.com (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Meta (META) and Tesla (TSLA)) lagged for the quarter as market participation broadened.
  • U.S. large cap growth stocks underperformed in the quarter, with the Russell 1000® Growth Index down nearly 10%. The pullback follows two exceptional years of returns for growth stocks and the period saw leadership reverse into international and value indexes. We continue to see structural long-term advantages in U.S. large cap growth equities and the relative valuation gap versus other equity classes has returned to normalized levels.

Portfolio Highlights

The NYLI Winslow Large Cap Growth Fund (the “Fund”) outperformed the benchmark for the quarter. The Communication Services, Industrials and Consumer Discretionary sectors were key drivers of relative performance, largely due to stock selection. The Information Technology sector was the largest detractor, primarily due to stock selection. Our lack of exposure to the Consumer Staples and Energy sectors also detracted.

Outperformance also reflected the waning influence that we had anticipated of the ‘Magnificent 7’. While the group does have some correlation, we believe it is becoming increasingly heterogeneous and are actively managing each position. In aggregate, our active weighting of these holdings contributed to relative performance in the quarter.

What Helped:

  • The Communication Services, Industrials and Consumer Discretionary sectors were key drivers of relative performance, largely due to stock selection.
  • The top relative contributor to performance for the quarter was global leader in audio streaming and media services Spotify Technology SA (SPOT). The company reported another strong quarter and issued guidance ahead of expectations. The management team has pivoted to focus on profitability, in addition to revenue growth, and the shift has led to higher operating margins and free cash flow generation.
  • Arthur J Gallagher & Co (AJG), a leading insurance broker for the middle market, announced solid quarterly results, and management reiterated its outlook for continued strong organic growth. The pending closing of its AssuredPartners acquisition is anticipated to secure mid-teens growth in the coming years.

What Hurt:

  • The Information Technology sector was the largest detractor, primarily due to stock selection. Our lack of exposure to the Consumer Staples and Energy sectors also detracted.
  • The top relative detractor was AppLovin Corp (APP), a mobile technology company that provides software and AI solutions to help businesses connect with their customers. The company reported strong results during the period and released guidance that appears to be conservative. In addition, the company announced the planned sale of its gaming business which would position the company as a pure-play advertising platform with a higher margin structure as it expands into other verticals. However, the stock detracted from relative performance as several short-seller reports, which management claims are misinformed, were released and higher growth companies faced sentiment headwinds in the period.
  • Electronic payments provider Visa Inc (V) detracted from performance due to our underweight position in the stock; however, the portfolio’s aggregated exposure to fintech led to positive relative performance. Visa continues to benefit from the secular shift to digital payments and a reduction in regulations under the new administration could be an additional catalyst.

data.title: ITT's Digital Drive: Sensors, Software, And The Path To Premium Margins
data.description:
Summary

ITT Inc. (NYSE:ITT) is an industrial compounder that is quietly blending its mission-critical hardware with high margin service and software offerings. Despite its legacy business exposed to cyclicality, its true growth runway lies in turning one-time sales into expanding recurring contracts. With breakthrough products like VIDAR smart motor and digital software sales, I believe its growth opportunities are not yet fully appreciated by the market. Thus, I rate this company a Buy.

Business Model

Essentially, ITT business is well-supported by three pillars. Firstly, its Motion Technologies division sells to automakers brake pads, shock absorbers and polymer gaskets which are seals that keep vehicle's fluids safely contained. Approximately half of the revenue from this division is recurring revenue, as they supply replacement parts (refer to page 6 of 2024 10-K). In fact, the aftermarket would carry higher margins because OEMs would carry volume discounts, but the aftermarket which are repair shops, and they tend to be willing to pay for a premium.

Their second pillar is Industrial Process, where ITT sells pump and valve systems to oil refineries and chemical manufacturers. Then, once these systems are installed, ITT can also provide spare parts and maintenance revenue. These oil and chemical companies require these valves because they need precise and reliable control over high-pressure fluids and ensure leaks do not happen. For instance, some of its customers include big integrated oil companies like ExxonMobil and Shell. ~43% of its revenue actually comes through its maintenance revenue (refer to page 7 of 2024 10-K). This ensures recurring maintenance cash flow.

Lastly, its third pillar is Connect & Control Technologies. Think of this division as a maker of "nerve endings" for machines. They design and manufacture small but also critical wiring harnesses, which are cables that carry power and data. They mainly serve from aviation fleet to automated factories. As sales to distributors represents 30% which is recurring, and I can deduce that the remaining 70% to direct OEMs also holds considerable % of revenue that is recurring. Thus, this is a testament that ITT holds tremendous branding power as companies seek quality and safety in these crucial parts.

As ITT operates services centers in 30+ countries, they are able to dispatch a trained technician within hours. This rapid response is actually a competitive advantage to maintain long duration contracts. To dive even deeper, ITT is not just selling metal parts. They are actually forging a relationship with their partners to provide essential parts but also, more importantly, mission-critical services via its technical expertise. Thus, it has built a sticky ecosystem that would lead to cash flow.

Investment Thesis

For the past two years, ITT is quietly transforming into a hybrid hardware-plus-software model. For instance, their pumps, valves, and connectors have embedded sensors which record data like vibration, pressure, and temperatures in real-time. These data would flow into ITT's cloud platform, where advanced analytics would use these data to flag any abnormality. Thus, it provides value as their customers can be proactive rather than reactive if there are any impending damages happening. Customers would be able to subscribe to this service at a modest annual fee, typically at ~2% original equipment cost (estimate by dividing $50k sensor with a $2mil pump).

This is also powerful because now clients can schedule repair and maintenance services, which would contribute to more cashflow ahead. As it is a software-as-a-service business, it is high-margin and strengthens the relationship with their customers. It is also a sticky business because once they changed this service provider, their insights would be lost.

Even though management did not disclose this, I suspect the revenue contribution is tiny by assuming the fees are ~2% of original equipment cost. However, this could easily grow quickly as by looking at other companies that have done similarly. Siemens has amassed over 1mil connected products which should have contributed to higher revenue growth as its Xcelerator platform enables it to grow revenue at a large clip. Although they are not exactly the same, it is still a cloud platform that provides hardware and digital services. Moreover, ITT could just tap onto their existing base as an add-on option which can be easily done, generating operating leverage. This also means that margin expansion would be quicker, as the software margins are higher.

Launched in Q1 2025, VIDAR is a smart motor that delivers up to 52% energy savings for centrifugal pumps and HVAC. Management has estimated that there is $6bn addressable market for these smart motors. If ITT can capture just 5% of them by 2027, that would drive further revenue growth. It would also make economic sense for clients as it generates energy savings, and it has minimal downtime in converting their motors to VIDAR. Thus, this ease of installation should encourage them to replace, generating further growth. Therefore, I believe that these 2 factors would further expand the company's growth potential.

Valuation

Currently, ITT trades at a premium to its peers and historical as well as the market has confidence in its quality business model and ability for margin expansion. On a forward Non-GAAP P/E, it is trading at ~24x which is ~24% premium above sector median, and forward EV/EBITDA at ~15x, which is ~34% premium to sector median.

This means that investors are actually willing to pay a premium. However, it also has a higher forward EBIT growth of ~10% which is ~38% greater than sector median. Thus, this explains that the company is trading at a premium. But this also leaves little room for error. Any slowdown in growth would quickly erode the premium, leading to de-rating.

However, knowing that the business is actively growing its SaaS-like software business. These high-growth SaaS business usually command a 6-10x EV/Sales multiple. As ITT is still effectively a hardware + small software plug, it would not reach that multiple. But over the few years, I suspect it should be able to reach at least 4x EV/Sales multiple for that growing software-like exposure. Thus, this has not been priced in.

Thus, I am comfortable in paying a premium for this company due to its growth potential and evolving quality business model is something that I am optimistic about.

Risk

ITT faces several risks as even though it has three pillar business, it is still succumbed to cyclicality from its auto and industrial capex, from weaker demand or pullback in energy investments. Competition within the space from larger rivals in pumps or connects may undercut ITT's price or provide a more innovative solution that rivals VIDAR.

Integration and execution also play a part which could dampen investor's confidence and lastly its premium valuation that is priced for perfection.

Conclusion

I think ITT's nascent predictive software business is not fully priced in by the market with its ability to expand margins quickly. The VIDAR product adoption is also another growth driver that the market is anticipating, which has tremendous addressable market to grow its business. Thus, ITT is more than just a cyclical parts supplier, but it is also a comparatively high return business which justifies its premium valuation. I rate this a long-term buy.

data.title: MST: Good For Trading, Not For Income
data.description:
Defiance Leveraged Long + Income MSTR ETF (NASDAQ:MST) is a new ETF, launched in May. About two months old, I wanted to take a look at it and see how it related to Strategy (MSTR), being a fund based on that stock. Moreover, what should the investor make of "Leveraged Long + Income" part of its name as they stumble upon it?

I found that it's much long-term or income-focused about it, and I rate it a Hold, thinking it too risky over time to be a good Buy.

Performance So Far

The first thing to note is the frequency of distributions. MST has paid weekly since its first dividend on May 20th.

Altogether, a single share has received $1.9178 in dividends since the launch.

Thus, some of the price decline since launch is a consequence of these distributions, but the fact that total returns are negative also indicates that the holdings themselves have been down recently.

What we are seeing, however, is that the fund prioritizes liquidity and monetization for its shareholders, so let's look at the strategy and portfolio behind this dividend policy.

Concept and Holdings

According to their prospectus, MST is an actively managed ETF that engages in derivative trades, in order to achieve daily returns of 150% to 200% of MSTR. While not a given, the fund may also use leverage in addition to their derivative position to achieve this goal. The choice of derivatives and timing of leverage are all subject to management's situational judgment.

In short, it's a fund that actively trades on the swings of MSTR. It also means that the holdings are highly dynamic. Thankfully, the manager's website gives an up-to-date display of their holdings.

The screenshot (above) from that page shows parts of the holdings, but more or less captures what happens inside this ETF. The derivatives vary between swap agreements and call options. The calls are staggered across several strike prices, in order to provide flexibility to the manager based on market movements. This includes creating call credit spreads, in order to generate income in the portfolio. The Treasury positions (either directly or through funds), meanwhile, serve as collateral for the derivative positions they take.

The image I've displayed is truly a snapshot, as per the prospectus:

At the end of each trading day, the Fund’s swaps and options are marked to market (valued based on current market prices), and the Fund’s investment adviser rebalances the portfolio to maintain leveraged exposure of approximately 150% to 200% of the Underlying Security’s share price.

Given this much activity, it's not surprising that distributions are regularly passed to shareholders. Nevertheless, management states that MST's primary investment objective is long-term capital appreciation, with income being secondary.

Future Outlook

The investment objective seems to suggest that MST is a fund that could be bought with long-term intentions, perhaps buying a large position and reinvesting the weekly dividends. While this would provide many opportunities of compounding, there are plenty of ways this can pan out badly for long-term investors.

Let's talk about the underlying security first: MSTR. I've covered it twice, and last time I rated it a Hold. While that was in February, I am not of a mind to update that rating. One can easily surmise that I won't think MST is a Buy if I don't think that about MSTR. I'll just quote my own closing remarks from there:

I take issue with the idea that selling shares at a premium to the portfolio to buy more creates "BTC yield," any more than Berkshire doing the same would create "KO yield" with more shares of Coca-Cola.

Without a source of earnings (something Berkshire has) to support this BTC accumulation, it's not clear why those who believe in BTC would aim for MSTR instead of buying the BTC directly. It just doesn't make sense to me.

For a long-term investor to see appeal in an ETF that has the long term as part of its investment objective, we need to be able to see the long-term potential in the underlying security, and I already find that dubious.

MST holding options and its leverage are both especially risky to long-term investors. The potential for magnified losses on even a 5% drop in BTC that gets locked in by daily rebalancing isn't something to ignore. The 1.31% expense ratio doesn't make recovery of principal much easier over time.

A similar income fund that I've covered before is YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY). It employs synthetic covered calls and has the effect of regularly passing through gains to shareholders. While it isn't without risks, the potential for losses aren't enhanced in their portfolio, and I didn't even find that to be a Buy.

In my view, MST is more a trader's security. MSTR effectively operates as a derivative of Bitcoin (BTC-USD), making MST a derivative of a derivative, potentially very volatile and risky.

Conclusion

I am initiating a Hold rating largely because I see no compelling case to think BTC will move up or down considerably in the near-term, which would trickle into more volatile reactions from the likes of MSTR and MST. In addition, a Buy rating on this security doesn't make sense for long-term investors. While the fund may declare long-term intentions, it seems to me the approach just doesn't allow for it.

data.title: MST: The Weekly Distribution Leveraged Fund On MSTR
data.description:
Have you ever seen a daily leveraged ETF that combines an income strategy like a credit call spread and distributes income weekly? Well, that’s essentially what the Defiance Leveraged Long + Income MSTR ETF (NASDAQ:MST) does. Let’s be honest: it’s not something you see every day. And I was really intrigued to see how some analysts are already talking about MST as an ETF offering indirect, income-generating exposure to Bitcoin.

Fast thinking: “wow.”

Slow thinking: “wait... what?!”

Curious, because if I let my imagination run wild, I immediately think of an MSTX with distributions like an MSTY. Then I come back to reality, and realize it’s quite different… but before that.

What is MST really?

A brand-new ETF from Defiance, launched on May 1, 2025, with just $88 million in AUM and 22 positions in its portfolio, which, as we’ll see, is essentially a piece of financial engineering architecture designed to “optimize” exposure to MSTR. Interestingly, Morningstar immediately categorized it under Trading – Miscellaneous, framing it as a tactical rather than strategic ETF, suited for a satellite rather than a core portfolio allocation, for short-term plays rather than long-term holdings (which, let me say, contrasts a bit with the mindset of an income investor).

First thought: “Okay, but how much does it cost me?” A fair and implicit question. The answer: an expense ratio of 1.31%, and a hidden cost of 0.39% spread (hopefully temporary), as indicated by the 30-day median spread.

But focusing on cost without understanding the value is a mistake, we know that well by now. So what value does MST offer in return for such a relatively high cost?

The Holdings

I prefer to start with the holding distribution, so I can get a sense of what MST is really made of, before being influenced by the marketing and fact sheets:

A dominant position: 180% in Total Return Swaps on MSTR (via Marex) + 2.65% via Clear Street. So it’s fair to say that MST gains leveraged exposure to MSTR through derivatives (TRS). Then comes the “income-oriented” flavor typical of option-based ETFs: credit call spreads on MSTR, short out-of-the-money calls, long further out-of-the-money calls. Then there are cash equivalents, 11% in Treasuries, etc., mainly serving as collateral (more than for stabilization).

The Strategy

Now let’s read the prospectus: what immediately strikes me is that the primary objective is long-term capital growth, and the secondary objective is to generate consistent income. It aims to provide a daily return equal to 150%-200% of MSTR’s performance. It also integrates a credit call spread strategy to generate weekly (or monthly) income.

The words here are extremely important, and I want to highlight them:

  • Income is secondary and not guaranteed. And the options strategy is applied to ~50% of net exposure.
  • TRS (Total Return Swaps) are reset daily: we enter the logic of leveraged ETFs, where in my opinion there should always be a very large disclaimer: daily leverage does not guarantee the same proportions over the long term. The risk? Return decay, just look at what’s happening to MSTX, which maintains similar leverage to MSTR.

A “Yielding Bitcoin”

The term Yielding Bitcoin is my own interpretation, Defiance doesn’t define it this way in the prospectus, although the marketing materials do refer to indirect exposure to Bitcoin, naturally because MSTR’s well-known strategy involves acquiring Bitcoin. I, personally, added the word yielding because MST includes up to 50% of net exposure in credit call spreads.

But what does it really make sense to expect in terms of price behavior? In my opinion, we can use the performance of MSTX as a benchmark, however, let’s keep in mind that MST has a cap on the upside movement, which could potentially cut capital gains performance, thus a leverage effect yes, but reduced (in my opinion) on the upside.

As for income, it’s unclear, not even the factsheets shed light on it. The only example I know well is MSTY, but that follows a completely different strategy, and I don’t think it makes sense to expect those levels of yield here; it’s not even the fund’s objective.

As of now, SA reports a TTM dividend yield of 9.92%, while Defiance shows a 30-day SEC yield of 1.22%, although the current distribution rate (which varies significantly) is listed at 78%. Here it’s crucial not to be misled, and for this reason, it lies at the core of the ...

Risk Section

First fear: NAV decay. Am I wrong to say that the investment strategies used by the managers of MST make it likely to generate a potentially high NAV decay?

  1. The 19a filings already show a return of capital distribution above 95%. Resulting in a distribution rate of 78%, which, let me say, is “a bit” misleading. Then there's the 30-day SEC yield, at 1.22%, which doesn’t account for option premiums, also creating a false perception of yield. The discrepancy makes the real size of the income unclear and, in my opinion, reinforces the risk of NAV decay.

  2. Am I wrong to say that MSTR’s volatility will not play in favor of MST? In practical terms, the fund sacrifices part of the upside to obtain weekly distributions, which should theoretically cushion the drawdowns. But I think this sort of “hedging” is not so evident when negative volatility is as high as MSTR’s (implied volatility above 50 points).

Conclusion

A fascinating financial engineering product that, in my opinion, should be considered a tactical allocation, definitely not a core holding (if I may add, by paying close attention to the bid-ask spread on short-term trades). After all, it’s essentially a leveraged derivative exposure to MSTR. Among the metrics to monitor, I definitely include the distributions, through the 19a filings, but also the options strategy, mainly whether it will be able to generate alpha compared to MSTX and MSTY.

data.title: ADP National Employment Report: 33K Private Jobs Unexpectedly Lost In June
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The economic mover and shaker this week is Friday's employment report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. This monthly report contains a wealth of data for economists, the most publicized being the month-over-month change in Total Nonfarm Employment.

The forecast for the forthcoming BLS report is that 110,000 jobs were added in June. However, each month a few days before we receive the highly anticipated jobs report, ADP releases their data on new nonfarm private jobs.

The ADP employment report revealed that 33,000 nonfarm private jobs were unexpectedly lost in June, down from the 29,000 addition in May. This is the first monthly reduction since March 2023 when there was a decline of 53,000 jobs. The latest reading was lower than the expected 99,000 addition.

Here is a visualization of the two series over the past twelve months. There is no correlation between the ADP and BLS employment report.

Here is an excerpt from today's ADP report press release:

"Though layoffs continue to be rare, a hesitancy to hire and a reluctance to replace departing workers led to job losses last month. Still, the slowdown in hiring has yet to disrupt pay growth."

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in the ADP headline number since the company's earliest published data with the new methodology in 2010. This is quite a volatile series, so we've plotted the monthly data points as dots along with a six-month moving average, which gives us a clearer sense of the trend. The latest six-month moving average is 79,000, the lowest level in nearly five years.

As we see in the chart above, the trend peaked in September 2015 and then went negative for the first time in late 2019, just before the NBER declared a recession start. The COVID-19 pandemic has brought employment numbers down to levels we have never seen this century. The trend reached a new high in 2021 at 875,000 and has recently dropped back to pre-pandemic levels.

ADP Employment: Industry

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into two categories: Goods Producing and Service Providing. The US is predominantly a services economy, so it comes as no surprise that Services employment has shown stronger jobs growth.

It makes sense that service-producing employment has plummeted during the pandemic for a couple of reasons - our economy is mostly supported by service-producing jobs, and during the pandemic, those same services were brought to a halt. In June, goods-producing jobs rose by 32,000 while service-providing jobs lost 66,000.

The sector with the largest growth in June was Leisure & Hospitality, with a gain of 32,000. The biggest loser last month was Professional & Business Services with a loss of 56,000 jobs, followed by Educational & Health Services with a loss of 52,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each industry's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Region

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into nine regions (census divisions). The region with the largest growth in June was the East South Central region, with a gain of 20,000 private jobs. Meanwhile, the West North Central region experienced the largest loss at -28,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each region's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Establishment Size

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into five sizes. Establishments with 500+ employees saw the largest growth in June, gaining 30,000 jobs, while those with 1-19 employees experienced the largest loss at -29,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each establishment size's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tesla's Problems Get Bigger And Bigger
data.description:
Article Thesis

Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) (TSX:TSLA:CA) reported its Q2 delivery numbers on Wednesday. Deliveries were down by 13.5%, missing the consensus estimate. The decline was slightly larger than the decline in Q1, and again, deliveries were way below production numbers.

I believe that this indicates that the Model Y refresh does not explain the poor performance, and it shows that Tesla is facing a substantial demand problem, not being able to sell all the vehicles it produces. The combination of declining demand, declining margins, and a very high valuation makes TSLA stock look unattractive, I believe.

Past Coverage

I have written about Tesla here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently when I covered the company's Q1 earnings report in April. With Tesla updating us on how it fared in the second quarter, at least when it comes to deliveries and production levels, it is time to take another look at the company today.

What Happened?

Tesla, Inc. has not yet announced its financial results for Q2 yet, but it has announced how many vehicles it produced and sold during the period, and what those numbers looked like for its energy storage business. Here are the important metrics:

  • Deliveries totaled 384,000, down 14% from one year earlier.
  • Tesla produced 410,000 vehicles during the quarter, with 397,000 of those being 3 and Y models, while only 13,000 were S, X, and Cybertruck vehicles.
  • Tesla deployed 9.6GWh of energy storage products during the quarter.

A 14% deliveries decline is pretty bad all by itself, but we have to take a more in-depth look to see what those numbers actually mean. After all, there can be more than one reason for lower delivery numbers.

What Do These Numbers Mean For Tesla?

It is, for example, possible that deliveries decline due to consumers being less interested in electric vehicles. But it does not look like that is the case -- competitors such as BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY, OTCPK:BYDDF) reported massive growth for the second quarter, so demand for EVs must be very healthy, especially in China, the world's largest EV market. Analysts see the global EV market growing by double digits this year; thus, there is no waning demand for EVs in general. Instead, demand for EVs keeps growing in multiple markets, thus the sales potential for EV companies is good. Overall weakness across the EV market does not explain why Tesla's deliveries are falling at a double-digit rate, both in Q1 and in Q2, with the Q2 decline even being slightly larger than the decline in Q1.

Of course, company-specific items can be at play as well. It is, for example, possible that a company experiences part shortages due to a supplier falling out. In that case, we could expect the company to see its production fall significantly, but if demand is healthy, the company will sell all the vehicles it produces, plus some of the vehicles it had in its inventory from previous quarters. In that scenario, the automobile company would be supply constrained -- due to part shortages or other production issues, it can't produce as many vehicles as the market wants. The same would hold true if a company's production is temporarily disrupted by retooling of production lines, model refreshes, and so on. But looking at the number of vehicles that Tesla produced and the number of vehicles it sold, it is pretty clear that this isn't the case, either. After all, Tesla produced way more vehicles than it managed to sell -- to me, this shows that Tesla is demand-constrained, not supply constrained.

Tesla can produce a lot of vehicles, but it isn't able to find willing buyers for its output. Hence, it produced more than it sold, which was also the case in Q1. In Q1, Tesla had already produced close to 30,000 more vehicles than it was able to sell, and in Q2, Tesla again produced close to 30,000 more vehicles than it was able to sell. Tesla, of course, also had thousands of vehicles in inventory at the beginning of the year -- but that number rose by between 50,000 and 60,000 vehicles in the first half of this year alone.

Earlier this year, many bulls argued that Tesla only experienced a sales decline due to the Model Y refresh. By that logic, the changes across its fabs, etc., have disrupted production, which is why Tesla wasn't able to sell as many vehicles as in previous quarters. But that does not fit at all with what we are seeing both in Q1 and Q2 -- production isn't the problem, demand is the problem. Inventory levels keep rising as more and more finished but unsold vehicles pile up.

The evening following the Q1 report, I was already pretty convinced that Tesla has a demand problem, but the Q2 report makes me even more sure about that. Where did this come from? There are several factors at play.

First, Tesla's model line-up isn't great -- many models are aged (despite refreshes, as those don't really change much about the vehicle), and Tesla's one pretty new model, the Cybertruck, doesn't really fit well with what most consumers want and has a somewhat dubious style.

Second, competition keeps growing, and many peers are getting stronger and stronger. The market for EVs is growing, but that doesn't mean that all companies that sell EVs will grow. Those with more attractive line-ups, better tech, better styles, and, importantly, better prices, will gain market share, while those with worse products and unattractive pricing will lose market share. Tesla faces tough competition in China, such as from BYD and Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACY), but it also faces mounting competition in Europe, such as from legacy player Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY), which grew its EV deliveries by more than 110% in Q1 (Q2 numbers aren't out yet, but likely showed strong growth as well).

Third, politics likely plays a role: CEO Elon Musk, whatever you think about how, is a controversial figure. It seems likely that he "turned off" some potential customers over the last year, which then hurts Tesla's sales potential. Especially when there are more and more alternatives as more and more companies enter the EV market and release more and more models, it is easy for those who don't like Elon Musk to shop elsewhere.

What Does This Mean For Tesla's Stock?

When a company faces demand problems in its by far the biggest segment -- automobiles in the case of Tesla -- then that's, of course, a negative for the stock. But it does not automatically mean that the company's shares will fall. A strong performance across other segments may offset this to some degree, for example. In Tesla's case, the energy business grew, but only very slightly. In Q2 of 2024, Tesla deployed 9.4 GWh of storage, while that number grew by 2% to 9.6 GWh in Q2 of 2025. A 2% growth rate in a smaller business unit does not offset a 14% decline in a much larger business unit, of course.

Tesla has plans in robotics, automobile driving, and so on, but so far, none of these units generate any meaningful revenue, and they are, of course, not generating any profits, either. The recent launch of "robotaxis" in Austin includes safety personnel on board, and suggests, I believe, that Tesla is way behind Waymo (GOOG, GOOGL).

None of this would be a problem if Tesla were trading at an undemanding valuation -- but at around 170x forward earnings, Tesla is a very expensive stock. NVIDIA (NVDA), which is growing at a gigantic pace, is trading at a mid-30s earnings multiple, for reference. Considering the weak operating performance, the lack of near-term catalysts, the ultra-high valuation, and the lack of shareholder returns, I believe that Tesla stock is quite unattractive today and that it has significant downside potential from where it trades today. This doesn't mean that the stock will necessarily fall, and I am not shorting it, but the risk-reward of owning it is not at all compelling, I think.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Atlantic Union Bankshares: Looking Cheap After The Sandy Spring Deal
data.description:
2025 has been a tumultuous year for America's smaller bank stocks. While the Wall Street players have lifted industry-tracking ETFs to a double-digit total return, regional banks have trailed that performance by some way. Within that group, Virginia-based Atlantic Union Bankshares (NYSE:AUB) has fared particularly poorly. Its stock is still down around 12% so far this year, albeit that represents a big improvement from its tariff-driven lows back in April.

I can't say for sure, but the bank's recent purchase of Sandy Spring Bancorp may have something to do with it. Specifically, I wonder whether the market is adopting a particularly cautious stance towards the bank's meaty commercial real estate ('CRE') exposure.

If so, I would be minded to take on that risk. Atlantic's shares may have rebounded off their April nadir, but that still only leaves them trading for 10.5x consensus 2025 EPS. Thanks to the Sandy deal, those earnings are forecast to grow strongly next year – and that puts the stock at more like 8.5x EPS based on 2026 consensus. That looks cheap next to peers, and it could drive a comfortable double-digit return over the next 12 months.

CRE Concerns To Blame?

Atlantic closed on its purchase of Sandy Spring back in April. Following this deal, the newly enlarged bank will have supplemented its existing mid-Atlantic footprint with exposure to the Washington metro area. It will have over $30 billion in total assets, over $25 billion in loans, and over $30 billion in total customer deposits.

Like I said above, Atlantic’s stock has trailed the regional bank space by some margin this year. Credit fears strike me as one logical reason for this, namely the bank's CRE exposure. To put some numbers on it, Atlantic was sitting on nearly $8 billion in non-owner occupied CRE loans at the end of last quarter, equal to a chunky 43% of its total loan book. Expressed relative to Tier 1 Capital and the bank’s allowance for loan losses (‘ALLL’), its non-owner occupied CRE concentration was around 330%. For reference, the average bank with over $10 billion in assets had roughly 30% of its loans in non-owner occupied CRE last quarter (relative to Tier 1 Capital and ALLL, non-owner occupied CRE concentration was around 195%).

Sandy Spring was even more into CRE loans. These topped 50% of its book just before its acquisition by Atlantic and over 400% relative to Tier 1 Capital plus its ALLL. Plus, with Sandy Spring, you also had the kicker of a heavy geographic concentration in the D.C. metro area.

If that is indeed the reason for Atlantic’s sluggish 2025 stock performance, there are grounds for optimism. For one, the bank has just offloaded $2 billion in CRE loans to help de-lever the post-deal balance sheet. Naturally, that will push its CRE concentration down.

Credit quality trends were also looking fine here. Atlantic’s net charge-offs were a meagre 0.05% of all loans last quarter, down around 8 basis points year-on-year.

Non-performing loans (‘NPL’) and earlier-stage delinquencies also look relatively modest. On the former, NPLs were around 0.41% of Atlantic’s total loans last quarter. While up 11 basis points on the year-ago period, that's hardly a disaster: the average NPL rate for banks with over $10 billion in assets was 0.82% last quarter. As for delinquencies, only 0.23% of Atlantic’s loans were 30–89 days past due. That was about flat year-on-year, and Atlantic was doing better than the industry on this metric as well.

Things also look solid if we go back a bit further. Between 2017 and 2024, for example, Atlantic's net charge-off rate averaged just 0.08%. That was around 13 basis points below the peer group average.

Driving A Discount

Atlantic’s recent financials are a little messy. Last quarter, the bank made $46.9 million in net income for its common shareholders, or $0.52 per share. The latter was down $0.10 on the year-ago period, mapping to a return on tangible common equity of roughly 12%. Similarly, analysts only expect non-GAAP EPS of $0.79 when Atlantic reports Q2 results later in the month.

I wouldn’t read too much into these numbers. Last quarter’s results were impacted by some merger-related expenses, while next quarter’s print won’t be reflective of the true earnings potential of the enlarged bank. Among other factors, cost savings will only start to feed through in the back-half of the year, while Atlantic’s share count will be much higher post-deal. Despite this, Atlantic stock still trades for just 10.5x 2025 consensus earnings per share, with analysts expecting earnings to push on towards the end of the year.

2026 should provide a more accurate reflection of Atlantic's business. This is when the bank will see the benefit from cost synergies arising from the Sandy Spring deal, not to mention the income accretion from fair value marks.

With that, analysts are currently pencilling in $3.82 per share in 2026 EPS. At Atlantic’s current stock price of $32.66, the associated price/earnings multiple would be just 8.5x. That compares favorably to a selection of similarly sized banks.

Compared to the same set, Atlantic's dividend is at a discount as well.

Let's say consensus remains unchanged, and the stock trades for 9.5x consensus EPS a year from now. That alone would drive a double-digit return – and that's not including the contribution from Atlantic's dividend. That's how cheap the stock appears to be.

Conclusion

2025 has so far been a lackluster year for Virginia's Atlantic Union Bankshares, with the stock trailing the wider regional bank space by nearly 15 points. While I can't be 100% sure, the bank's credit concentration may be partly to blame, particularly as it has just bought an even more CRE-centric bank in Sandy Spring Bancorp. Be that as it may, credit quality was looking robust as of the most recent quarter we have date for, while Atlantic's longer-term track record also looks encouraging.

Whatever the reason, the stock's sluggish start to 2025 has left the Atlantic trading for just 8.5x consensus 2026 EPS. That looks cheap next to peers, as does the 4.2% forward dividend yield. More importantly, those figures don't require the bank to move mountains to drive a double-digit total return over the next year. For that reason, I am bullish.
